texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: On maximum-sum matchings of bichromatic points
texts.text: On maximum-sum matchings of bichromatic points

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Huemer et al. (Discrete Math, 2019) proved that for any two finite point sets R and B in the plane with | R | = | B | , the perfect matching that matches points of R with points of B , and maximizes the total squared Euclidean distance of the matched pairs, has the property that all the disks induced by the matching have a nonempty common intersection. A pair of matched points induces the disk that has the segment connecting the points as diameter. In this note, we characterize these maximum-sum matchings for some family of continuous (semi)metrics, focusing on both the Euclidean distance and squared Euclidean distance. Using this characterization, we give a different but simpler proof for the common intersection property proved by Huemer et al..
texts.text: Huemer et al. (Discrete Math, 2019) proved that for any two finite point sets R and B in the plane with | R | = | B | , the perfect matching that matches points of R with points of B , and maximizes the total squared Euclidean distance of the matched pairs, has the property that all the disks induced by the matching have a nonempty common intersection. A pair of matched points induces the disk that has the segment connecting the points as diameter. In this note, we characterize these maximum-sum matchings for some family of continuous (semi)metrics, focusing on both the Euclidean distance and squared Euclidean distance. Using this characterization, we give a different but simpler proof for the common intersection property proved by Huemer et al..

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let R and B be two point sets in the plane with | R | = | B | . The points in R are called red , and those in B are called blue . A matching M of R ∪ B is a partition of R ∪ B into n pairs such that each pair consists of a red point and a blue point. A point p ∈ R and a point q ∈ B are matched if and only if the (unordered) pair ( p, q ) is in the matching. For every p, q ∈ R 2 , we use pq to denote the segment connecting p and q , ℓ ( pq ) to denote the line through pq , and B ( pq ) to denote the disk with diameter equal to the length ∥ p -q ∥ of pq , that is centered at the midpoint ( p + q ) / 2 of pq . For any matching M , we use B M to denote the set of the disks associated with, or induced by, the matching, that is, B M = {B ( pq ) : ( p, q ) ∈ M} , where B ( pq ) is the disk induced by the edge ( p, q ).
texts.text: Let R and B be two point sets in the plane with | R | = | B | . The points in R are called red , and those in B are called blue . A matching M of R ∪ B is a partition of R ∪ B into n pairs such that each pair consists of a red point and a blue point. A point p ∈ R and a point q ∈ B are matched if and only if the (unordered) pair ( p, q ) is in the matching. For every p, q ∈ R 2 , we use pq to denote the segment connecting p and q , ℓ ( pq ) to denote the line through pq , and B ( pq ) to denote the disk with diameter equal to the length ∥ p -q ∥ of pq , that is centered at the midpoint ( p + q ) / 2 of pq . For any matching M , we use B M to denote the set of the disks associated with, or induced by, the matching, that is, B M = {B ( pq ) : ( p, q ) ∈ M} , where B ( pq ) is the disk induced by the edge ( p, q ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Given a metric, or semi-metric function d : R 2 × R 2 → R ≥ 0 , we say that a matching M is maxsum if it maximizes ∑ ( p,q ) ∈M d ( p, q ) among all matchings of R and B . Recall that a function is a semi-metric if it satisfies all the properties of a metric function except the triangle inequality.
texts.text: Given a metric, or semi-metric function d : R 2 × R 2 → R ≥ 0 , we say that a matching M is maxsum if it maximizes ∑ ( p,q ) ∈M d ( p, q ) among all matchings of R and B . Recall that a function is a semi-metric if it satisfies all the properties of a metric function except the triangle inequality.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Huemer et al. [8] proved that if M is a max-sum matching for d ( p, q ) = ∥ p -q ∥ 2 for all p, q ∈ R 2 , then all the disks of B M have a point in common. That is, d is the squared Euclidean distance, also called quadrance , and it is in fact a semi-metric.
texts.text: Huemer et al. [8] proved that if M is a max-sum matching for d ( p, q ) = ∥ p -q ∥ 2 for all p, q ∈ R 2 , then all the disks of B M have a point in common. That is, d is the squared Euclidean distance, also called quadrance , and it is in fact a semi-metric.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the Euclidean distance d ( p, q ) = ∥ p -q ∥ , Bereg et al. [4] proved that any max-sum matching M between R and B does not always satisfy that all disks in B M have a common point, although the disks intersect pairwise. As their main result, they proved that the common intersection is always
texts.text: For the Euclidean distance d ( p, q ) = ∥ p -q ∥ , Bereg et al. [4] proved that any max-sum matching M between R and B does not always satisfy that all disks in B M have a common point, although the disks intersect pairwise. As their main result, they proved that the common intersection is always

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this note, we consider max-sum matchings between planar colored point sets R and B with | R | = | B | = n , n ≥ 1, for any continuous (semi-)metric d of the Euclidean plane satisfying the following properties:
texts.text: In this note, we consider max-sum matchings between planar colored point sets R and B with | R | = | B | = n , n ≥ 1, for any continuous (semi-)metric d of the Euclidean plane satisfying the following properties:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: P1. d ( x, y ) ≥ 0 for all x, y ∈ R 2 .
texts.text: P1. d ( x, y ) ≥ 0 for all x, y ∈ R 2 .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: P2. d ( x, x ) = 0 for all x ∈ R 2 .
texts.text: P2. d ( x, x ) = 0 for all x ∈ R 2 .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: P3. d ( x, y ) = d ( y, x ) for all x, y ∈ R 2 .
texts.text: P3. d ( x, y ) = d ( y, x ) for all x, y ∈ R 2 .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: P4. For any fixed points p, q ∈ R 2 with p = q , and any real constant t , the level set { z ∈ R 2 : d ( p, z ) -d ( q, z ) = t } is connected.
texts.text: P4. For any fixed points p, q ∈ R 2 with p = q , and any real constant t , the level set { z ∈ R 2 : d ( p, z ) -d ( q, z ) = t } is connected.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that the first three properties are the standard properties of any semi-metric, whereas the fourth property is satisfied by semi-metrics such as the Euclidean distance and the squared Euclidean distance.
texts.text: Note that the first three properties are the standard properties of any semi-metric, whereas the fourth property is satisfied by semi-metrics such as the Euclidean distance and the squared Euclidean distance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section 2, we characterize the max-sum matchings between three red points and three blue points in terms of the common intersection of certain sets defined by the six points. Using this characterization, in the case where d is the Euclidean quadrance, we give in Section 3 an elementary proof to the main theorem by Huemer et al. [8]: The disks induced by a max-sum matching have a common point.
texts.text: In Section 2, we characterize the max-sum matchings between three red points and three blue points in terms of the common intersection of certain sets defined by the six points. Using this characterization, in the case where d is the Euclidean quadrance, we give in Section 3 an elementary proof to the main theorem by Huemer et al. [8]: The disks induced by a max-sum matching have a common point.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Motivated by the study of geometric matchings of planar point sets, Huemer et al. [8] proved that for any finite bichromatic point set R ∪ B with | R | = | B | , and a max-sum matching M of R and B according to the squared Euclidean distance, all disks in B M have a nonempty common intersection. Later, Bereg et al. [4] proved that all disks in B M have a nonempty common intersection for any point set P of 2 n uncolored points in the plane, when M is a max-sum matching of P with respect to the Euclidean distance. This result has been slightly strengthened by Barabanshchikova and Polyanskii [3], who proved that the interiors of all disks in B M have a nonempty common intersection in the case where all elements of P are distinct.
texts.text: Motivated by the study of geometric matchings of planar point sets, Huemer et al. [8] proved that for any finite bichromatic point set R ∪ B with | R | = | B | , and a max-sum matching M of R and B according to the squared Euclidean distance, all disks in B M have a nonempty common intersection. Later, Bereg et al. [4] proved that all disks in B M have a nonempty common intersection for any point set P of 2 n uncolored points in the plane, when M is a max-sum matching of P with respect to the Euclidean distance. This result has been slightly strengthened by Barabanshchikova and Polyanskii [3], who proved that the interiors of all disks in B M have a nonempty common intersection in the case where all elements of P are distinct.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sober´ on and Tang [11] considered this kind of geometric intersection problems in the more general context of geometric graphs: Given a geometric graph with vertex set a finite point set in the plane (where the edges are straight segments connecting points), they define the graph as a Tverberg graph if the disks induced by the edges of the graph have a nonempty common intersection. They show that for any odd planar point set there exists a Hamiltonian cycle which is a Tverberg graph, and that for any even planar point set there exists a Hamiltonian path with the same property. Notice that the previous mentioned results can be stated in terms of Tverberg graphs. For example, the result of Huemer et al. [8]: For any finite bichromatic point set R ∪ B with | R | = | B | , any max-sum matching of R and B according to the squared Euclidean distance is a Tverberg graph.
texts.text: Sober´ on and Tang [11] considered this kind of geometric intersection problems in the more general context of geometric graphs: Given a geometric graph with vertex set a finite point set in the plane (where the edges are straight segments connecting points), they define the graph as a Tverberg graph if the disks induced by the edges of the graph have a nonempty common intersection. They show that for any odd planar point set there exists a Hamiltonian cycle which is a Tverberg graph, and that for any even planar point set there exists a Hamiltonian path with the same property. Notice that the previous mentioned results can be stated in terms of Tverberg graphs. For example, the result of Huemer et al. [8]: For any finite bichromatic point set R ∪ B with | R | = | B | , any max-sum matching of R and B according to the squared Euclidean distance is a Tverberg graph.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pirahmad et al. [10] refined and extended these results. They proved, for example, that: for any finite point set in the plane there exists a Hamiltonian cycle that is a Tverberg graph; for any even
texts.text: Pirahmad et al. [10] refined and extended these results. They proved, for example, that: for any finite point set in the plane there exists a Hamiltonian cycle that is a Tverberg graph; for any even

texts.label: text
texts.orig: point set in R d there exists a matching that is a Tverberg graph; and for any red and blue points in R d , d ≥ 3, there exists a perfect red-blue matching that is a Tverberg graph. This last result generalizes the initial result of Huemer et al. [8] to higher dimensions.
texts.text: point set in R d there exists a matching that is a Tverberg graph; and for any red and blue points in R d , d ≥ 3, there exists a perfect red-blue matching that is a Tverberg graph. This last result generalizes the initial result of Huemer et al. [8] to higher dimensions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the same direction, Abu-Affash et al. [1] proved that for any finite planar point set P , the maximum-weight spanning tree of P is a Tverberg graph. In fact, they proved that the center of the smallest enclosing circle of P is contained in all the disks induced by the tree.
texts.text: In the same direction, Abu-Affash et al. [1] proved that for any finite planar point set P , the maximum-weight spanning tree of P is a Tverberg graph. In fact, they proved that the center of the smallest enclosing circle of P is contained in all the disks induced by the tree.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fingerhut (see Eppstein [5]), motivated by a problem in designing communication networks (see Fingerhut et al. [6]), conjectured that given a set P of 2 n uncolored points in the plane, not necessarily distinct, and a max-sum matching { ( a i , b i ) , i = 1 , . . . , n } of P , there exists a point o of the plane, not required to be a point of P and called the center of the matching, such that

texts.orig: Recently, Barabanshchikova and Polyanskii [2] confirmed the conjecture of Fingerhut. After that, P´ erez-Lantero and Seara [9] proved the bichromatic version of the main result obtained by Bereg et al. [4]: If R and B are two point sets in the plane with | R | = | B | = n , and M = { ( r i , b i ) ∈ R × B : i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n } is a max-sum matching for the Euclidean distance, there exists a point o of the plane such that

After that, P´ erez-Lantero and Seara [9] proved the bichromatic version of the main result obtained by Bereg et al. [4]: If R and B are two point sets in the plane with | R | = | B | = n , and M = { ( r i , b i ) ∈ R × B : i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n } is a max-sum matching for the Euclidean distance, there exists a point o of the plane such that

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These results about the conjecture of Fingerhut are related to the common intersection of convex sets induced by the pairs of the max-sum matchings, ellipses in this case. Indeed, for two points p, q ∈ R 2 and a real number λ ≥ 1, let E λ ( pq ) denote the (region bounded by) the ellipse with foci p and q and major axis length λ ∥ p -q ∥ . That is, E λ ( pq ) = { x ∈ R 2 : ∥ p -x ∥ + ∥ q -x ∥ ≤ λ ∥ p -q ∥} . The statement of Equation (1) is equivalent to stating that the common intersection of the ellipses E 2 / √ 3 ( a 1 b 1 ) , E 2 / √ 3 ( a 2 b 2 ) , . . . , E 2 / √ 3 ( a n b n ) is nonempty. Furthermore, the statement of Equation (2) is equivalent to stating that the common intersection of the ellipses E √ 2 ( r 1 b 1 ), E √ 2 ( r 2 b 2 ) , . . . , E √ 2 ( r n b n ) is also nonempty [2, 5, 9].
texts.text: These results about the conjecture of Fingerhut are related to the common intersection of convex sets induced by the pairs of the max-sum matchings, ellipses in this case. Indeed, for two points p, q ∈ R 2 and a real number λ ≥ 1, let E λ ( pq ) denote the (region bounded by) the ellipse with foci p and q and major axis length λ ∥ p -q ∥ . That is, E λ ( pq ) = { x ∈ R 2 : ∥ p -x ∥ + ∥ q -x ∥ ≤ λ ∥ p -q ∥} . The statement of Equation (1) is equivalent to stating that the common intersection of the ellipses E 2 / √ 3 ( a 1 b 1 ) , E 2 / √ 3 ( a 2 b 2 ) , . . . , E 2 / √ 3 ( a n b n ) is nonempty. Furthermore, the statement of Equation (2) is equivalent to stating that the common intersection of the ellipses E √ 2 ( r 1 b 1 ), E √ 2 ( r 2 b 2 ) , . . . , E √ 2 ( r n b n ) is also nonempty [2, 5, 9].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 Characterization of max-sum matchings of 6 points
texts.text: 2 Characterization of max-sum matchings of 6 points

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let R = { a, b, c } be a set of three red points and B = { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } a set of three blue points. Let d be a continuous (semi-)metric function on R 2 satisfying properties P1, P2, P3, and P4. Let { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } be a matching of R and B . Let us define the following six sets:
texts.text: Let R = { a, b, c } be a set of three red points and B = { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } a set of three blue points. Let d be a continuous (semi-)metric function on R 2 satisfying properties P1, P2, P3, and P4. Let { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } be a matching of R and B . Let us define the following six sets:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: H ( a, b ) = { x ∈ R : d ( a, b ) -d ( b, b ) ≤ d ( a, x ) -d ( b, x ) } H ( b, c ) = { x ∈ R 2 : d ( b, c ′ ) -d ( c, c ′ ) ≤ d ( b, x ) -d ( c, x ) } ,

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 1: Definition of the sets H ( a, b ) and h ( a, b ) for (a) ∥ · ∥ , and (b) ∥ · ∥ 2 (the labels b ′ and a ′ are used to indicate that they are in the boundaries of H ( a, b ) and h ( a, b ), respectively).
texts.text: Figure 1: Definition of the sets H ( a, b ) and h ( a, b ) for (a) ∥ · ∥ , and (b) ∥ · ∥ 2 (the labels b ′ and a ′ are used to indicate that they are in the boundaries of H ( a, b ) and h ( a, b ), respectively).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In other words, H ( a, b ) is the set of the (blue) points x ∈ R 2 such that d ( a, b ′ ) + d ( b, x ) ≤ d ( a, x ) + d ( b, b ′ ). That is, { ( a, x ) , ( b, b ′ ) } is a max-sum matching of { a, b } and { x, b ′ } . Similarly, h ( a, b ) is the set of the (blue) points x ∈ R 2 such that { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, x ) } is a max-sum matching of { a, b } and { a ′ , x } . For example, if d is the Euclidean distance, then the boundaries of the sets H ( a, b ) and h ( a, b ) are arcs of hyperbolas with foci a and b (see Figure 1a). Similarly, if d is the squared Euclidean distance, then H ( a, b ) and h ( a, b ) are half-planes whose boundaries are perpendicular to the line ℓ ( ab ) through the segment ab (see Figure 1b).
texts.text: In other words, H ( a, b ) is the set of the (blue) points x ∈ R 2 such that d ( a, b ′ ) + d ( b, x ) ≤ d ( a, x ) + d ( b, b ′ ). That is, { ( a, x ) , ( b, b ′ ) } is a max-sum matching of { a, b } and { x, b ′ } . Similarly, h ( a, b ) is the set of the (blue) points x ∈ R 2 such that { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, x ) } is a max-sum matching of { a, b } and { a ′ , x } . For example, if d is the Euclidean distance, then the boundaries of the sets H ( a, b ) and h ( a, b ) are arcs of hyperbolas with foci a and b (see Figure 1a). Similarly, if d is the squared Euclidean distance, then H ( a, b ) and h ( a, b ) are half-planes whose boundaries are perpendicular to the line ℓ ( ab ) through the segment ab (see Figure 1b).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 2.1. If the five intersections H ( a, b ) ∩ H ( b, c ) ∩ H ( c, a ) , h ( a, b ) ∩ h ( b, c ) ∩ h ( c, a ) , H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ) , H ( b, c ) ∩ h ( b, c ) , and H ( c, a ) ∩ h ( c, a ) are all nonempty, then { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } is a max-sum matching of { a, b, c } and { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } .
texts.text: Lemma 2.1. If the five intersections H ( a, b ) ∩ H ( b, c ) ∩ H ( c, a ) , h ( a, b ) ∩ h ( b, c ) ∩ h ( c, a ) , H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ) , H ( b, c ) ∩ h ( b, c ) , and H ( c, a ) ∩ h ( c, a ) are all nonempty, then { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } is a max-sum matching of { a, b, c } and { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Therefore, { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } is a max-sum matching of { a, b, c } and { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } , since its total sum is never smaller than that of any other matching.
texts.text: Therefore, { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } is a max-sum matching of { a, b, c } and { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } , since its total sum is never smaller than that of any other matching.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 2.2. If { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } is a max-sum matching of { a, b, c } and { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } , then the five intersections H ( a, b ) ∩ H ( b, c ) ∩ H ( c, a ) , h ( a, b ) ∩ h ( b, c ) ∩ h ( c, a ) , H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ) , H ( b, c ) ∩ h ( b, c ) , and H ( c, a ) ∩ h ( c, a ) are all nonempty.
texts.text: Lemma 2.2. If { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } is a max-sum matching of { a, b, c } and { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } , then the five intersections H ( a, b ) ∩ H ( b, c ) ∩ H ( c, a ) , h ( a, b ) ∩ h ( b, c ) ∩ h ( c, a ) , H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ) , H ( b, c ) ∩ h ( b, c ) , and H ( c, a ) ∩ h ( c, a ) are all nonempty.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Observe that { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) } must be a max-sum matching of { a, b } and { a ′ , b ′ } . Then, we have that d ( a, b ′ ) + d ( b, a ′ ) ≤ d ( a, a ′ ) + d ( b, b ′ ), which is equivalent to d ( a, b ′ ) -d ( b, b ′ ) ≤ d ( a, a ′ ) -d ( b, a ′ ). This implies that a ′ , b ′ ∈ H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ), which means that H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ) is not empty. Analogously, H ( b, c ) ∩ h ( b, c ) and H ( c, a ) ∩ h ( c, a ) are not empty.
texts.text: Proof. Observe that { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) } must be a max-sum matching of { a, b } and { a ′ , b ′ } . Then, we have that d ( a, b ′ ) + d ( b, a ′ ) ≤ d ( a, a ′ ) + d ( b, b ′ ), which is equivalent to d ( a, b ′ ) -d ( b, b ′ ) ≤ d ( a, a ′ ) -d ( b, a ′ ). This implies that a ′ , b ′ ∈ H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ), which means that H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ) is not empty. Analogously, H ( b, c ) ∩ h ( b, c ) and H ( c, a ) ∩ h ( c, a ) are not empty.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us prove now that H ( a, b ) ∩ H ( b, c ) ∩ H ( c, a ) = ∅ . The proof for h ( a, b ) ∩ h ( b, c ) ∩ h ( c, a ) is analogous. We divide the proof into two cases.

texts.text: Let U, V ⊂ R 2 be two closed sets with nonempty connected boundaries. It is a known result from point-set topology that if the three sets U \ V , U ∩ V , and ∂V \ U are all nonempty, then there exists a point z ∈ ∂U ∩ ∂V .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 2.3. The matching { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } is a max-sum matching of the red points { a, b, c } and the blue points { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } if and only if the five intersections H ( a, b ) ∩ H ( b, c ) ∩ H ( c, a ) , h ( a, b ) ∩ h ( b, c ) ∩ h ( c, a ) , H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ) , H ( b, c ) ∩ h ( b, c ) , and H ( c, a ) ∩ h ( c, a ) are all nonempty.
texts.text: Theorem 2.3. The matching { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } is a max-sum matching of the red points { a, b, c } and the blue points { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } if and only if the five intersections H ( a, b ) ∩ H ( b, c ) ∩ H ( c, a ) , h ( a, b ) ∩ h ( b, c ) ∩ h ( c, a ) , H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ) , H ( b, c ) ∩ h ( b, c ) , and H ( c, a ) ∩ h ( c, a ) are all nonempty.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 Common intersection for the Euclidean quadrance
texts.text: 3 Common intersection for the Euclidean quadrance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let R and B be two point sets in the plane, with | R | = | B | = n ≥ 2, and let M be a max-sum matching of R and B with respect to the Euclidean quadrance. Huemer et al. [8] first proved that the disks in B M intersect pairwise, so the common intersection of the disks in B M holds for n = 2. To give our new proof of this property for n ≥ 3, we will use Helly's Theorem [7] in R 2 , that is, we will prove the common intersection for n = 3, by using the characterization of Section 2. For completeness, we state Helly's Theorem:
texts.text: Let R and B be two point sets in the plane, with | R | = | B | = n ≥ 2, and let M be a max-sum matching of R and B with respect to the Euclidean quadrance. Huemer et al. [8] first proved that the disks in B M intersect pairwise, so the common intersection of the disks in B M holds for n = 2. To give our new proof of this property for n ≥ 3, we will use Helly's Theorem [7] in R 2 , that is, we will prove the common intersection for n = 3, by using the characterization of Section 2. For completeness, we state Helly's Theorem:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 3.1 (Helly [7]) . Let F be a finite family of closed convex sets in R m such that every subfamily of m + 1 sets of F has nonempty intersection. Then, all sets in F have nonempty intersection.
texts.text: Theorem 3.1 (Helly [7]) . Let F be a finite family of closed convex sets in R m such that every subfamily of m + 1 sets of F has nonempty intersection. Then, all sets in F have nonempty intersection.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let M = { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } be a max-sum matching of R = { a, b, c } and B = { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } , and let us define the three sets S ( a, b ) = H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ), S ( b, c ) = H ( b, c ) ∩ h ( b, c ), and S ( c, a ) = H ( c, a ) ∩ h ( c, a ). Given that S ( a, b ), S ( b, c ), and S ( c, a ) are nonempty strips perpendicular to the lines ℓ ( ab ), ℓ ( bc ), and ℓ ( ca ), respectively, Theorem 2.3 asserts that S ( a, b ) ∩ S ( b, c ) ∩ S ( c, a ) = ∅ (see Figure 2a). So, let z be a point of S ( a, b ) ∩ S ( b, c ) ∩ S ( c, a ).
texts.text: Let M = { ( a, a ′ ) , ( b, b ′ ) , ( c, c ′ ) } be a max-sum matching of R = { a, b, c } and B = { a ′ , b ′ , c ′ } , and let us define the three sets S ( a, b ) = H ( a, b ) ∩ h ( a, b ), S ( b, c ) = H ( b, c ) ∩ h ( b, c ), and S ( c, a ) = H ( c, a ) ∩ h ( c, a ). Given that S ( a, b ), S ( b, c ), and S ( c, a ) are nonempty strips perpendicular to the lines ℓ ( ab ), ℓ ( bc ), and ℓ ( ca ), respectively, Theorem 2.3 asserts that S ( a, b ) ∩ S ( b, c ) ∩ S ( c, a ) = ∅ (see Figure 2a). So, let z be a point of S ( a, b ) ∩ S ( b, c ) ∩ S ( c, a ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For two distinct points p, q ∈ R 2 , let τ ( pq ) be the ray with apex p that goes through q . For three distinct points p, q, r ∈ R 2 , let ω ( pqr ) denote the convex wedge bounded by τ ( pq ) and τ ( pr ) and ∆ pqr denote the triangle with vertices p , q , and r .
texts.text: For two distinct points p, q ∈ R 2 , let τ ( pq ) be the ray with apex p that goes through q . For three distinct points p, q, r ∈ R 2 , let ω ( pqr ) denote the convex wedge bounded by τ ( pq ) and τ ( pr ) and ∆ pqr denote the triangle with vertices p , q , and r .

texts.orig: Proposition 3.2. Let z ∗ be the orthogonal projection of the point z into the line ℓ ( ab ) . The following statements are satisfied: (i) If z ∗ ∈ ab , then z ∗ ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) . (ii) If z ∗ ∈ τ ( ab ) \ ab , then z ∗ , b ∈ B ( aa ′ ) . (iii) If z ∗ ∈ τ ( ba ) \ ab , then z ∗ , a ∈ B ( bb ′ ) .
texts.text: Proposition 3.2. Let z ∗ be the orthogonal projection of the point z into the line ℓ ( ab ) . The following statements are satisfied: (i) If z ∗ ∈ ab , then z ∗ ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) . (ii) If z ∗ ∈ τ ( ab ) \ ab , then z ∗ , b ∈ B ( aa ′ ) . (iii) If z ∗ ∈ τ ( ba ) \ ab , then z ∗ , a ∈ B ( bb ′ ) .

Hence: (i) If z ∗ ∈ ab , then z ∗ ∈ aa ∗ ∩ bb ∗ , which implies z ∗ ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) (see Figure 2b). (ii) If z ∗ ∈ τ ( ab ) \ ab , then z ∗ , b ∈ aa ∗ , which implies z ∗ , b ∈ B ( aa ′ ) (see Figure 2c). (iii) If z ∗ ∈ τ ( ba ) \ ab , then z ∗ , a ∈ bb ∗ , which implies z ∗ , a ∈ B ( bb ′ ).

Hence: (i) If z ∗ ∈ ab , then z ∗ ∈ aa ∗ ∩ bb ∗ , which implies z ∗ ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) (see Figure 2b). (ii) If z ∗ ∈ τ ( ab ) \ ab , then z ∗ , b ∈ aa ∗ , which implies z ∗ , b ∈ B ( aa ′ ) (see Figure 2c). (iii) If z ∗ ∈ τ ( ba ) \ ab , then z ∗ , a ∈ bb ∗ , which implies z ∗ , a ∈ B ( bb ′ ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We prove first the extreme case in which a , b , and c are collinear points. Assume w.l.o.g. that b belongs to the segment ac . Since S ( a, b ) ∩ S ( b, c ) ∩ S ( c, a ) = ∅ , this intersection must be a line through b ′ perpendicular to ℓ ( ac ). So, let z ∗ be the intersection of this line with ℓ ( ac ), and note that z ∗ ∈ B ( bb ′ ). If z ∗ ∈ ac , then z ∗ ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( cc ′ ) by Proposition 3.2 (i). This implies that z ∗ ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) ∩ B ( cc ′ ). If z ∗ ∈ τ ( ac ) \ ac , then c ∈ B ( bb ′ ) since c ∈ bz ∗ , and c ∈ B ( aa ′ ) by Proposition 3.2 (ii). Hence, c ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩B ( bb ′ ) ∩B ( cc ′ ). Similarly, if z ∗ ∈ τ ( ca ) \ ac , then a ∈ B ( bb ′ ) since a ∈ bz ∗ , and a ∈ B ( cc ′ ) by Proposition 3.2 (iii). Hence, a ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩B ( bb ′ ) ∩B ( cc ′ ). In all the cases, we have that B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) ∩ B ( cc ′ ) = ∅ .
texts.text: We prove first the extreme case in which a , b , and c are collinear points. Assume w.l.o.g. that b belongs to the segment ac . Since S ( a, b ) ∩ S ( b, c ) ∩ S ( c, a ) = ∅ , this intersection must be a line through b ′ perpendicular to ℓ ( ac ). So, let z ∗ be the intersection of this line with ℓ ( ac ), and note that z ∗ ∈ B ( bb ′ ). If z ∗ ∈ ac , then z ∗ ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( cc ′ ) by Proposition 3.2 (i). This implies that z ∗ ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) ∩ B ( cc ′ ). If z ∗ ∈ τ ( ac ) \ ac , then c ∈ B ( bb ′ ) since c ∈ bz ∗ , and c ∈ B ( aa ′ ) by Proposition 3.2 (ii). Hence, c ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩B ( bb ′ ) ∩B ( cc ′ ). Similarly, if z ∗ ∈ τ ( ca ) \ ac , then a ∈ B ( bb ′ ) since a ∈ bz ∗ , and a ∈ B ( cc ′ ) by Proposition 3.2 (iii). Hence, a ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩B ( bb ′ ) ∩B ( cc ′ ). In all the cases, we have that B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) ∩ B ( cc ′ ) = ∅ .

texts.text: Proposition 3.3. If the point z belongs to the wedge ω ( abc ) (resp. ω ( bca ) , ω ( cab ) ), then z is contained in B ( aa ′ ) (resp. B ( bb ′ ) , B ( cc ′ ) ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. We prove the case z ∈ ω ( abc ). The proofs for the other cases are analogous. Given that z ∈ h ( a, b ) ∩ H ( c, a ) and that z ∈ ω ( abc ), the lines ∂h ( a, b ) and ∂H ( c, a ) cut perpendicularly the rays τ ( ab ) and τ ( ac ), respectively. Let u = τ ( ab ) ∩ ∂h ( a, b ) and v = τ ( ac ) ∩ ∂H ( c, a ), which are the orthogonal projections of a ′ into τ ( ab ) and τ ( bc ), respectively (see Figure 3a). Furthermore, z is in one of the right triangles ∆ aa ′ u and ∆ aa ′ v , both contained in B ( aa ′ ). Hence, z ∈ B ( aa ′ ).
texts.text: Proof. We prove the case z ∈ ω ( abc ). The proofs for the other cases are analogous. Given that z ∈ h ( a, b ) ∩ H ( c, a ) and that z ∈ ω ( abc ), the lines ∂h ( a, b ) and ∂H ( c, a ) cut perpendicularly the rays τ ( ab ) and τ ( ac ), respectively. Let u = τ ( ab ) ∩ ∂h ( a, b ) and v = τ ( ac ) ∩ ∂H ( c, a ), which are the orthogonal projections of a ′ into τ ( ab ) and τ ( bc ), respectively (see Figure 3a). Furthermore, z is in one of the right triangles ∆ aa ′ u and ∆ aa ′ v , both contained in B ( aa ′ ). Hence, z ∈ B ( aa ′ ).

We also have that z ∗ ∈ B ( aa ′ ) by using Proposition 3.3. In fact, z ∗ belongs to the convex quadrilateral of vertices a , u , a ′ , and v (see Figure 3b). Hence, we have that z ∗ ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) ∩ B ( cc ′ ). Otherwise, if z ∗ / ∈ bc , say z ∗ ∈ τ ( bc ) \ bc w.l.o.g., then c ∈ av which implies c ∈ B ( aa ′ ) (see Figure 3c). Hence, we have that z ∗ ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) ∩ B ( cc ′ ). Otherwise, if z ∗ / ∈ bc , say z ∗ ∈ τ ( bc ) \ bc w.l.o.g., then c ∈ av which implies c ∈ B ( aa ′ ) (see Figure 3c). texts.orig: Case 3: The point z is not contained in any of the wedges ω ( abc ), ω ( bca ), and ω ( cab ). Assume w.l.o.g. that z is such that c ∈ ∆ abz (see Figure 3d). The orthogonal projection of z into ℓ ( ac ) belongs to τ ( ac ) \ ac , so c ∈ B ( aa ′ ) by Proposition 3.2. Similarly, the orthogonal projection of z into ℓ ( bc ) belongs to τ ( bc ) \ bc , so c ∈ B ( bb ′ ) by Proposition 3.2. Hence, c ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩B ( bb ′ ) ∩B ( cc ′ ). In all of the cases, we can find a point in the intersection B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) ∩ B ( cc ′ ).
texts.text: Case 3: The point z is not contained in any of the wedges ω ( abc ), ω ( bca ), and ω ( cab ). Assume w.l.o.g. that z is such that c ∈ ∆ abz (see Figure 3d). The orthogonal projection of z into ℓ ( ac ) belongs to τ ( ac ) \ ac , so c ∈ B ( aa ′ ) by Proposition 3.2. Similarly, the orthogonal projection of z into ℓ ( bc ) belongs to τ ( bc ) \ bc , so c ∈ B ( bb ′ ) by Proposition 3.2. Hence, c ∈ B ( aa ′ ) ∩B ( bb ′ ) ∩B ( cc ′ ). In all of the cases, we can find a point in the intersection B ( aa ′ ) ∩ B ( bb ′ ) ∩ B ( cc ′ ).
